data.name: index.md
content: This projects folder is the original reason any of this exists. Long before there was an About page or an Experience section explaining any of it, there was just a personal website meant to hold whatever I'd built and finished. That impulse came first. Every other folder on this site, the writing, the résumé framing, the whole idea of a "portfolio," got built up around it afterward, not the other way around.

So if the rest of the site is me explaining myself, this is the part where I stop talking. It's the closest thing here to the actual shape of how I think: pick a problem, build until it works, ship it somewhere real. Whatever I end up doing for a living, it'll look a lot more like what's in this folder than like any page describing it.
Which is to say: everything below actually runs. Not screenshots, not write-ups pretending to be demos — real, working software you can click into and use.

It's flat by design: pick anything below and open it. The one exception is Websites, holding the three sites in this network — this portfolio, a separate art portfolio for my digital art, and a music site. All three are built the same way: plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, zero frameworks, zero build step, sharing one design system down to the theme, palette, CRT effect, and footer chrome — recently put through a real accessibility and performance audit rather than just a glance at the score.

>[ deeper dive ]

All three sites recently went through a real Lighthouse audit, not just a glance at the score. A few of the bugs it turned up were the kind a passing eye test would never catch. The home page's animated ASCII logo was reading its container's width from the DOM on every single animation frame, right after mutating that frame's text — forcing a synchronous layout recalculation dozens of times a second, fixed by caching the width and only re-reading it on an actual resize.

The art site had a color that couldn't actually win: its error red was reused for text on both a light bar and a dark card background, and no single shade clears accessible contrast against both at once — the math for one is the literal inverse of the math for the other. Split into two shades, one per background, so it still reads as one consistent "alert red" even though it's technically two different values now.

The music site had a sneakier one: several nav links used display: contents so a letter and title could sit in separate grid columns without a wrapping box breaking the grid layout — clean for layout, but a boxless element also reports a zero-size tap target, which fails every touch-target accessibility check no matter how big the visible text looks. Rebuilt with CSS subgrid instead, so the links keep a real, full-row hit box while their children still land in the exact same columns. Its two fonts also moved off Google's CDN and onto self-hosted copies of the exact same files, cutting a 3-hop cross-origin request chain down to direct, parallel fetches.

And a handful of images across the sites were flat-out oversized for what they're actually used for — a logo sampled down to a 450-column ASCII canvas was still shipping at native 2400×1200 resolution, gallery art was going out at full painting resolution when the largest it's ever displayed is a 900px-wide lightbox. Resized everything to match its real usage instead of its source file.
data.closingQuote: Writing about the work is useful. This is where the work actually lives.

children.name: aaroncheung.me
content: This is the site you're standing in, built with plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript — zero frameworks, zero libraries, zero build step. Almost every behavior on it is code I wrote; the two exceptions are a form-delivery API behind the contact form and a lightweight, cookieless analytics script, each handling one job that isn't worth reinventing.
And since you're already looking at it: here's a live, real copy of this exact site, running inside itself, or view the source on GitHub.
aaroncheung.me

>[ deeper dive ]

Under the hood it runs like a small app. Every word of content lives in JSON files and gets rendered onto the page by hand-rolled scripts, so updating the site means editing data, not markup. Pages share layout through a tiny partials system I wrote instead of importing one.

index.html, css/, js/, data/, fonts/, partials/   -- this site
art/                                              -- digital art site
sound/                                            -- music essay site
projects/                                         -- embedded live demos

And if you're on a keyboard, press Ctrl+K: there's a fuzzy-search command palette, and the whole site can be driven without touching a mouse.

The theming goes deeper than a dark mode toggle. Color palettes, font switching, a CRT effect for the full retro terminal feel, and reduced-motion support for visitors who need it, all persisted in localStorage so the site remembers your setup between visits. Even the default font is self-hosted, so on a fresh load nothing phones home.

It's never finished, and that's deliberate. The experience section tells the story of how this site started and why it looks the way it does; this page is just where it currently stands. It grows whenever I learn something worth adding.

children.name: Art Portfolio
content: A collection of my digital art, presented as a screen that's quietly falling apart. Every piece lives inside a draggable window styled like an OS error dialog, scattered over a full-bleed background — drag them around, and the rest of the page glitches and flickers on its own, like the machine underneath is slowly failing.
Here's a live, real copy of the art site, running right inside this one, or view the source on GitHub.
aaroncheung.me/art/

>[ deeper dive ]

Each error window is a peephole, not its own image: one full-size copy of the artwork sits behind the whole scene, and every window just crops and counter-positions itself over a slice of it, so dragging windows around feels like exposing different parts of one hidden picture rather than moving separate copies.

The nav reads like a little process list — the current page renders as an info box, the other three sit as "virus alert" links offering to run. Clicking one flips which page is "active" and re-flows the other three back into alerts. The gallery page swaps that whole peephole scene for a flood of the same error-window chrome instead, one per piece, click any of them to zoom into a lightbox.

The ambient glitching (random flashes, plus a cursor-reactive Canvas mesh grid warping away from the pointer) started here and later moved into the main portfolio too, gated behind the shared CRT toggle so it reads as part of that effect rather than its own separate setting. Footer, theme/palette/font, and CRT/motion state are all shared with the main site through a small partials system — two sites that look nothing alike, running on one settings system underneath.

children.name: Music Memoir
content: A personal essay built like a print music magazine — big, bold serif display type, letters that swap from hollow to solid, hard diagonal splits between light and dark panels. Five chapters spell out SOUND, each pairing a real story about learning piano and violin — first by lesson, later by ear — with actual cited research on why it played out that way.
Here's a live, real copy of the music site, running right inside this one, or view the source on GitHub.
aaroncheung.me/sound/

>[ deeper dive ]

The whole thing runs on two fonts doing very different jobs: a heavy serif (Fraunces) for anything meant to read as a headline, and a condensed sans (Oswald) for the smaller editorial furniture — mastheads, folios, kickers. The cover and closing page both spell a word out in giant cascading letters, one solid and the rest hollow outlines, and every entry is color-blocked light or dark rather than blending between the two — bold, simple choices instead of subtle ones.

On desktop, a normal vertical scroll wheel drives the page sideways instead of down, eased into a smooth glide rather than jumping tick to tick; on mobile the whole rig steps aside for a plain vertical page with native anchor scrolling. Each of the five chapters — S, O, U, N, D — covers one stretch of actually learning music: getting dragged into piano lessons as a kid, resenting the drilling, quitting, picking it back up on my own terms years later, and teaching myself by ear instead of by sheet music. Every entry pairs that story with a real citation, actual psychology and music-education research on practice, motivation, and ear training, so the essay and the research sit right next to each other instead of off in a footnote.

Built entirely in HTML and CSS — no canvas, no images, just type. Like the art site, it shares its footer and theme/palette/font/CRT/motion settings with the main portfolio through the same small partials system — three sites that look nothing alike, running on one settings system underneath.

data.name: Jumpy
content: Jumpy is a small 2-player board game I built an AI opponent for, and you can play it below. The board is a row of 8 squares, and each side has 2 pieces that start at opposite ends. On your turn, you move one of your pieces one step toward the other side. If the next square is taken, your piece hops over it (and anything else in the way) and lands on the first open square it finds. The goal is simple: get both of your pieces off the far end before your opponent does.

There's one twist: if your hop lands you past exactly one of your opponent's pieces, that piece gets knocked all the way back near its own starting side. Racing ahead carelessly can backfire, and a well-timed hop can send your opponent's progress back to square one (almost literally). Full source on GitHub.

>[ deeper dive ]

The game came out of my AI coursework at UTD, where the actual assignment was to prompt ChatGPT into producing a good algorithm for it. I liked the game too much to leave it there, so I built the whole thing for real: the game, and an opponent worth playing.

The opponent's core is a bitboard. The entire game state lives in a single 16-bit integer, two bits per square, which means generating and testing moves is just bitwise operations: shifts and masks instead of loops over arrays. On top of that sits an iterative deepening search with a heuristic evaluation, so the bot searches as deep as its time allows and always has a best move ready.

Normally all of that is invisible, which is a shame, so I made it visible. Flip on "watch it think" above and the search log shows you the bot's actual deliberation: the moves it considers, how deep it looks, and what it decides. Fair warning before you start: it looks like a toy. Try beating it anyway.

data.name: Cursor Heatmap
content: A cursor heatmap is a visual map of where someone's mouse moved, lingered, and clicked on a page. Busier areas show up as "hotter" colors, quiet areas stay cool. They're normally used to see which parts of a page actually grab attention. This is a live, working version of that idea, built right into this page.

Move your mouse around the box below and watch the heatmap build up in real time: hovering or moving over an area adds a bit of heat, lingering in one spot for a while adds more, and clicking leaves a brighter, more concentrated mark. Colors shift from cool blue at light activity up to warm orange and yellow where you've spent the most time. Nothing here is recorded or sent anywhere — it's purely a live visual effect that lives and dies in your browser tab. Full source on GitHub.
This demo tracks real cursor movement, so it needs a mouse — open this page on a desktop browser to try it out.

>[ deeper dive ]

Under the hood, every movement, pause, and click paints a soft, low-opacity circle onto a hidden canvas; overlapping circles build up intensity the same way layering translucent paint gets darker. That intensity is then remapped through a color gradient and redrawn to the visible canvas several times a second, which is what gives the live, glowing heatmap effect.

This demo is actually the second life of this project. The original was a full pipeline: a tracking script streaming cursor events to a Node.js service, with Python processing them into the heatmap. Rebuilding it for this site, I distilled the whole idea down to something that runs entirely in your browser, which is also what makes the privacy promise above possible.

Two details I like. Activity isn't weighted equally: a click deposits about six times the heat of a passing move, and lingering builds its own slow burn, so the final picture reflects intent, not just motion. And the coloring runs through a precomputed 256-color gradient lookup table, which keeps remapping every pixel cheap enough to do several times a second.

data.name: Database Design
content: This started as UTD database coursework: design a relational database for a fictional company that manufactures products, procures parts from vendors, and manages a workforce across departments. The deliverable was the full stack of database work: ER modeling, a normalized schema, table creation and data insertion scripts, and a battery of fifteen queries against it.

ER Diagram — entities, attributes, and relationships
Relational Schema — normalized table layout
Entity Diagram (dbdiagram.io) — page 1
Entity Diagram (dbdiagram.io) — page 2

Most coursework like this dies in a submission folder. I deployed mine instead: every query button below is real, and the last row goes further, letting you insert a customer, list the table, and delete them again. Full schema and source on GitHub.

Interviewers in the Hellen Cole interview for job 11111
Jobs posted by Sales in January 2011
Employees with no supervisees
Marketing sites with no sales in March 2011
Jobs still unfilled one month after posting
Salesmen who sold every product type priced above $200
Departments with no job posts between Jan 1 and Feb 1, 2011
Existing employees who applied for job 12345
Best-selling product type by items sold
Product type with the highest net profit
Employees who have worked in every department
Name and email of a selected interviewee
Interviewees selected for every job they applied for
Employee with the highest average monthly salary
Cheapest vendor for Cup parts under 4 pounds

>[ deeper dive ]

The database runs live on this site's server, fronted by a PHP API — every button fires the real query against the real MySQL database and prints what comes back, including a full write path for the insert/delete row.

The queries are where it gets interesting, because several of them are the classically hard kind. "Employees who have worked in every department" and "salesmen who sold every product type above $200" are relational division problems — the sort of query that looks simple in English and takes real thought in SQL. Others chain through the schema: tracing which vendor supplies the cheapest part under a weight limit, or finding jobs still unfilled a month after posting.

data.name: ASCII Art Converter
content: A browser-based tool that converts any PNG or JPG into ASCII art, entirely client-side. No uploads, no server. Drop in an image and it samples every pixel's brightness and alpha channel, mapping each one to a character from the selected palette.

Five character palettes give the output different visual textures: classic density ramp (.:-=+*#%@), Unicode block fills (░▒▓█), thin verticals (·:;!|), circular shapes (.oO0), and binary (01). Full source on GitHub.

[ drop image or click to upload ]
cols200brightness0

>[ deeper dive ]

Of everything on this site, this is the most literal collision of the two things I do. I drew this site's logo myself, then ran the drawing through this tool, and the ASCII version it produced is what greets you on the home page. The site's identity is, quite literally, my art passed through my code.

Under the hood, the image is drawn onto a hidden HTML canvas at reduced resolution, with the column count you pick setting the horizontal detail. A correction factor accounts for monospace characters being roughly twice as tall as they are wide, so the output looks properly proportioned instead of vertically stretched. Transparent pixels become spaces, which keeps silhouettes clean for logos and icons. And brightness isn't a naive RGB average: it uses a perceptual luma formula, weighting green most and blue least, the way human eyes actually do.

data.name: Twitter / X Automation
content: This is a bot that ran once a day and posted to the X (Twitter) account where I share my digital art, no manual posting needed. Each day it had to make a small decision: post a brand-new piece, or re-share an older one to keep the account active. I'd queue up new art by dropping a file into a folder, and the bot checked that queue every time it ran. Here's the core decision function — the part that decides whether today is a new-art day or a retweet day:

def should_post_new_art():
    """Post new art if something's queued and the last few posts were mostly
    retweets -- otherwise fall back to retweeting an older piece."""
    if not load_queue():
        return False

    recent = load_recent_posts()[-RECENT_WINDOW:]
    if not recent:
        return True

    retweet_ratio = recent.count('retweet') / len(recent)
    return retweet_ratio > 0.5
Account credentials and the real posting history aren't included for obvious reasons, but the rest of the bot — login, queueing, captioning, and posting — is real: view source on GitHub.

>[ deeper dive ]

The decision logic above is simple but deliberate: if the last several posts had mostly been retweets and there was new art waiting in the queue, it would post the new piece. Otherwise, it would retweet whichever older piece had gone the longest without being re-shared, so nothing got forgotten.

For captions, the bot asked OpenAI to write like an art influencer announcing a new piece: short, enthusiastic, no fluff. Then it appended a handful of hashtags pulled from a curated list I maintained.

The biggest challenge was learning to drive Selenium and OpenAI together efficiently. Twitter's official API would have been the simpler route, and I knew it. I wanted browser automation precisely because it was the harder one: actually driving a real browser, logging in, waiting on dynamic elements, and clicking through the post flow like a human would. It's the closest I've come to seeing the web from the other side, how pages render, how sessions authenticate, and how fragile selectors punish assumptions.

data.name: Lofi Generator
content: Lofi Generator is a procedural lofi beat generator with no pre-recorded track underneath it — every chord, melody line, and drum hit is chosen live by a real functional harmony engine and rendered through the raw Web Audio API, so it plays forever without ever looping the same phrase twice. Press play below.

Rain, a thunderstorm, and four other ambience beds (Cityscape, Forest, Ocean, Fireplace) all layer independently of each other and of the music itself, alongside a vinyl-crackle toggle for room tone. Piano and drum hits are real recorded samples ported from meel-hd/lofi-engine (MIT licensed); everything else — the harmony, the melody, every ambience bed — is synthesized from scratch.

READYSTOPPED
100%
Full mixer
Instruments
100%
100%
100%
Rain
100%
Atmosphere
100%
Vinyl crackle
100%

>[ deeper dive ]

The chord progression isn't fixed: seven diatonic chords sit behind a real functional-harmony transition table (which chords are allowed to follow which), weighted by recency so the same chord doesn't keep coming back around too soon. Melody phrases walk the scale in chord-tone-aware steps — capped at a small jump size — across four subdivision lengths (half/quarter/eighth/sixteenth notes), so the phrasing itself varies bar to bar, not just the notes.

Getting the ambience beds to actually sound like weather rather than static took the most iteration of anything on this site. Early passes on the rain sounded like individual taps instead of a downpour; the fix ended up being a second, quieter continuous layer under the audible drops, not just turning the drops down. The forest atmosphere went through a similar arc — a twig-snap effect kept getting swallowed by the wind bed until the two were pulled into genuinely separate frequency bands instead of just being balanced by volume.

The player also runs site-wide: a small toggle in the footer keeps it playing across every page on this site, not just this one. Since a normal link click reloads the page (this isn't a single-page app), the trick is that the toggle's state is saved and the generator restarts fresh on the next page rather than literally continuing — and because it's procedural instead of a fixed track, a restart sounds identical to a continuation.
